assertion_results.text: Migration does NOT use a bare ALTER TABLE RENAME COLUMN as the primary strategy (may mention it as unsafe alternative)
assertion_results.evidence: Uses dual-write with add column + trigger, no bare RENAME

assertion_results.text: Migration uses a dual-write or copy-column strategy: adds new column, backfills, migrates application code, then drops old column
assertion_results.evidence: Phase 1 add/backfill/trigger, Phase 2 deploy app, Phase 3 drop old

assertion_results.text: A complete rollback migration is provided that reverses the changes
assertion_results.evidence: rollback.sql drops trigger, function, and column

assertion_results.text: The migration addresses zero-downtime by ensuring both old and new columns coexist during transition
assertion_results.evidence: Sync trigger keeps both columns in sync during transition

assertion_results.text: Verification queries are included that confirm data was correctly copied from email to email_address
assertion_results.evidence: Runbook: SELECT count(*) WHERE email_address IS NULL expecting 0

assertion_results.text: Data backup commands are included before any destructive operations
A deployment runbook is provided with phased steps (add column, backfill, deploy app changes, drop old column)
assertion_results.evidence: Runbook has Phase 1, Phase 2, Phase 3, and Rollback

assertion_results.text: The migration mentions or addresses the need to update application code, background jobs, or queries that reference the old column name
assertion_results.evidence: Migration header: 'Consumers to update: app code, background jobs, reporting, API contracts'
